title: Rabbit abscesses: recognition and treatment
subtitle: Why rabbit abscesses differ from those in other animals, and why draining is rarely the answer.
excerpt: Rabbit abscesses are notoriously difficult to treat because rabbit pus is thick, caseous, and does not drain the way it does in cats or dogs. The management options are surgical removal, marsupialisation, or euthanasia for large internal abscesses. Early detection and veterinary intervention give the best outcomes.
sourceNotes: British Veterinary Association, rabbit abscess clinical guidance. Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, health factsheets. Journal of Exotic Pet Medicine, rabbit abscess management review.

glossaryTerms.definition: A localised bacterial infection forming a walled pocket of pus in a rabbit's skin, jaw, limb, or internal organs. Rabbit pus is thick, white, and cheesy in texture rather than liquid; this makes simple lancing and drainage ineffective.

A surgical technique for rabbit abscesses in which the abscess is opened, the wall sutured to the surrounding skin to create an open pocket, and the cavity flushed and packed regularly until it heals from the inside. Avoids removing a large tissue mass and allows ongoing wound management.

content.text: A lump on a rabbit is almost always an abscess, and

behave differently from abscesses in most other domestic animals. Rabbit pus is thick and paste-like, not liquid. You cannot drain it with a needle or by lancing and squeezing. The bacterium most commonly responsible is Pasteurella multocida, with Staphylococcus aureus and other organisms also present.

The most common location is the jaw, neck, and behind the ear, where dental disease or fight wounds provide bacterial entry points. Dental root abscesses are particularly common in rabbits with molar spurs or overgrown teeth. Check the jaw and neck for firm, non-painful swellings as part of the weekly health check. Abscesses also appear at injection sites and along the spine or hindquarters in rabbits with fight injuries from other colony members.

Any firm lump that does not move freely under the skin, or that is attached to the jaw or a limb, requires veterinary assessment. The vet may perform fine-needle aspirate to confirm the contents and assess the bacterial load before deciding on surgical or medical management.
is the treatment of choice for accessible skin abscesses; surgical excision is preferred when the abscess can be removed cleanly. Both require anaesthesia.

After marsupialisation, flush the open pocket daily with dilute chlorhexidine and pack with manuka honey or antibiotic gauze. Keep this up for several weeks until the cavity heals from the inside. Slow work, but often effective for jaw abscesses.

Spinal, lung, or thoracic abscesses in rabbits are rarely amenable to surgical treatment and carry a poor prognosis. A rabbit with rapid weight loss, hind-limb weakness, or laboured breathing alongside a known abscess elsewhere should be assessed by a vet for internal spread. The welfare decision in these cases often involves euthanasia.
